section.title: Daniel's Prayer and the Seventy Weeks
section.sectionRationale: Reading in Jeremiah that the exile would last seventy years, Daniel pours out one of Scripture's great prayers of confession, pleading God's mercy for His people and city. God answers through Gabriel with the prophecy of the seventy weeks, a sweeping timetable of what God will accomplish for sin and for His holy city, culminating in an Anointed One who makes atonement and is cut off. It joins heartfelt repentance to the deepest promise of redemption in the book.
section.thesis: Daniel confesses his people's sin and appeals only to God's mercy and covenant faithfulness, and God answers not merely with release from Babylon but with the promise of seventy weeks in which an Anointed Prince will finish transgression, atone for iniquity, and bring in everlasting righteousness, though He is cut off to do it.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Daniel reads Jeremiah and turns to prayer; the confession of sin; the plea for mercy for God's own sake; Gabriel comes swiftly with an answer; and the prophecy of the seventy weeks and the Anointed One who is cut off.

verses.text: In the first year of Darius son of Xerxes, a Mede by descent, who was made ruler over the kingdom of the Chaldeans,
in the first year of his reign, I, Daniel, understood from the sacred books, according to the word of the LORD to Jeremiah the prophet, that the desolation of Jerusalem would last seventy years.
So I turned my attention to the Lord God to seek Him by prayer and petition, with fasting, sackcloth, and ashes.
And I prayed to the LORD my God and confessed, “O, Lord, the great and awesome God, who keeps His covenant of {{chesed|loving devotion}} to those who love Him and keep His commandments,
we have sinned and done wrong. We have acted wickedly and rebelled. We have turned away from Your commandments and ordinances.
We have not listened to Your servants the prophets, who spoke in Your name to our kings, leaders, fathers, and all the people of the land.

To You, O Lord, belongs righteousness, but this day we are covered with shame, the men of Judah, the people of Jerusalem, and all Israel near and far, in all the countries to which You have driven us because of our unfaithfulness to You.
O LORD, we are covered with shame, our kings, our leaders, and our fathers, because we have sinned against You.
To the Lord our God belong compassion and forgiveness, even though we have rebelled against Him
and have not obeyed the voice of the LORD our God to walk in His laws, which He set before us through His servants the prophets.

All Israel has transgressed Your law and turned away, refusing to obey Your voice; so the oath and the curse written in the Law of Moses the servant of God has been poured out on us, because we have sinned against You.
You have carried out the words spoken against us and against our rulers by bringing upon us a great disaster. For under all of heaven, nothing has ever been done like what has been done to Jerusalem.
Just as it is written in the Law of Moses, all this disaster has come upon us, yet we have not sought the favor of the LORD our God by turning from our iniquities and giving attention to Your truth.
Therefore the LORD has kept the calamity in store and brought it upon us. For the LORD our God is righteous in all He does; yet we have not obeyed His voice.

units.label: The plea for mercy for God's own sake
units.note: not for our righteousness, but Your compassion

verses.text: Now, O Lord our God, who brought Your people out of the land of Egypt with a mighty hand, and who made for Yourself a name renowned to this day, we have sinned; we have acted wickedly.

O Lord, in keeping with all Your righteous acts, I pray that Your anger and wrath may turn away from Your city Jerusalem, Your holy mountain; for because of our sins and the iniquities of our fathers, Jerusalem and Your people are a reproach to all around us.
So now, our God, hear the prayers and petitions of Your servant. For Your sake, O Lord, cause Your face to shine upon Your desolate sanctuary.

Incline Your ear, O my God, and hear; open Your eyes and see the desolation of the city that bears Your name. For we are not presenting our petitions before You because of our righteous acts, but because of Your great compassion.
O Lord, listen! O Lord, forgive! O Lord, hear and act! For Your sake, O my God, do not delay, because Your city and Your people bear Your name.”
While I was speaking, praying, confessing my sin and that of my people Israel, and presenting my petition before the LORD my God concerning His holy mountain,
while I was still praying, Gabriel, the man I had seen in the earlier vision, came to me in swift flight about the time of the evening sacrifice.
He instructed me and spoke with me, saying: “O Daniel, I have come now to give you insight and understanding.
At the beginning of your petitions, an answer went out, and I have come to tell you, for you are highly precious. So consider the message and understand the vision:

units.label: The prophecy of the seventy weeks
units.note: atonement and the Anointed One cut off

verses.text: {{shavuim|Seventy weeks}} are decreed for your people and your holy city to stop their transgression, to put an end to sin, to make {{kaphar|atonement}} for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness, to seal up vision and prophecy, and to anoint the Most Holy Place.

Know and understand this: From the issuance of the decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem until the {{mashiach|Messiah}}, the Prince, there will be seven weeks and sixty-two weeks. It will be rebuilt with streets and a trench, but in times of distress.

Then after the sixty-two weeks the Messiah will be cut off and will have nothing. Then the people of the prince who is to come will destroy the city and the sanctuary. The end will come like a flood, and until the end there will be war; desolations have been decreed.

And he will confirm a covenant with many for one week, but in the middle of the week he will put an end to sacrifice and offering. And on the wing of the temple will come the abomination that causes desolation, until the decreed destruction is poured out upon him.”

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love, mercy, and loyalty. Daniel appeals to the God who keeps His covenant of chesed, resting his whole prayer not on Israel's worthiness but on God's faithful, unbreakable love. It is the ground of hope for a guilty people: God's own loyal love.

shavuim.gloss: Sevens, or weeks. Seventy shavuim, seventy sevens, are decreed for Daniel's people and city. The word answers Jeremiah's seventy years and stretches them into a longer, symbolic span of God's redemptive plan, reaching its center in the work of the Anointed One.

kaphar.gloss: To make atonement, to cover or wipe away sin, to reconcile. At the heart of the seventy weeks is the promise to make kaphar for iniquity, the deepest goal of the whole prophecy. It points beyond the temple sacrifices to the once-for-all atonement accomplished by the Anointed One who is cut off.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed One, Messiah, the one set apart with oil for God's service, especially as king or priest. The prophecy names the coming of the Messiah, the Prince, who will then be cut off, and Christians read this as pointing directly to Jesus, the Anointed One who dies to atone for sin.

basic.context: Daniel, now an old man, is reading Jeremiah and realizes the seventy years of exile are nearly over. Rather than simply wait, he throws himself into prayer, confessing the sins of his people with deep humility and pleading for mercy, not because they deserve it but because of God's compassion and His own name. As he prays, the angel Gabriel arrives with an answer far greater than release from Babylon: the prophecy of the seventy weeks, God's timetable to deal with sin once and for all and to bring in everlasting righteousness through an Anointed One who is cut off.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter has two halves that belong together: a model prayer of confession and a dense prophecy. Daniel prays Scripture back to God, resting entirely on grace. The seventy weeks are among the most debated verses in the Bible, and faithful Christians differ on the exact scheme. What is clear, and central, is the sixfold goal of verse 24, finishing sin and making atonement, fulfilled in the Messiah who is cut off.
basic.originalLanguages: Daniel appeals to the God of chesed, covenant loving devotion. The answer is seventy shavuim, sevens, in which God will make kaphar, atonement, for iniquity. And the prophecy names the mashiach, the Anointed One, the Messiah who comes and is then cut off.
basic.typology: The daily and yearly sacrifices, and the Day of Atonement's covering of sin, all point to the atonement promised here, which the Messiah accomplishes in His death. The desolate sanctuary Daniel weeps over gives way to the true and final cleansing achieved when the Anointed One is cut off to atone for iniquity.
basic.christ: The seventy weeks reach their goal in the Messiah, the Prince, who is cut off but not for Himself. Jesus is the Anointed One whose death makes atonement for iniquity and brings in everlasting righteousness, exactly the six things verse 24 promises. Daniel's plea for God to forgive for His own sake is answered supremely at the cross.
basic.god: God is righteous in all His judgments, yet rich in compassion and forgiveness, keeping covenant love with an undeserving people. He hears prayer swiftly, answers beyond what is asked, and is working a plan that runs through the centuries to deal with sin forever. He acts for His own name's sake, which is the surest ground of hope.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 25 and 29 give the seventy years Daniel is reading. Leviticus 16 describes the atonement the Messiah fulfills. And Matthew 24 and Hebrews 9 pick up the ending of sacrifice and the once-for-all atonement of Christ.
basic.oneStory: Daniel's confession voices the whole exile's need: a sinful people who can appeal only to grace. God's answer lifts their eyes past release from Babylon to the real deliverance, the Anointed One who finishes transgression and atones for sin. The seventy weeks trace the line from exile straight to the cross, where the deepest desolation is answered by everlasting righteousness.

context.literary: The chapter pairs a prayer of national confession, among the finest in Scripture, with an angelic prophecy, showing that Daniel's visions are born in prayer. It stands at the heart of the visions, between the ram and goat of chapter 8 and the long final vision of chapters 10 to 12, and it deepens the theme of the desecrated and restored sanctuary with the promise of atonement.
context.historical: Daniel prays around 539 BC, as Persian rule begins and the seventy years of Jeremiah near their end; the decree to rebuild Jerusalem would come under Persian kings. The prophecy's language of a destroyed city and sanctuary was fulfilled again in AD 70, when Rome destroyed Jerusalem and the temple, which many connect with the people of the prince who is to come.
context.canonical: Daniel 9 gathers the Bible's covenant theology, drawing on Leviticus and Deuteronomy's blessings and curses, Jeremiah's seventy years, and the sacrificial system, and focuses it on the coming atonement. The abomination that causes desolation is taken up by Jesus, and the once-for-all end of sacrifice is expounded in Hebrews, making this chapter a bridge from the old covenant to the work of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model humble, grace-based confession and intercession for God's people, and to reveal God's answer, that beyond the end of exile He has decreed a longer plan to finish sin and make atonement through the Anointed One, giving exiles a hope far deeper than mere political restoration.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Daniel's prayer is a pattern to imitate, honest confession resting on God's mercy. The seventy weeks prophecy describes God's decreed plan; it is not a license for date-setting. The fixed point for all readers is verse 24's goal, accomplished in Christ, held with humility about the finer chronological details.

debates.question: How should the seventy weeks be understood?

views.label: Fulfilled in Christ's first coming
views.case: The messianic reading runs the weeks from a Persian decree to rebuild Jerusalem to the coming and cutting off of the Messiah; the sixfold goal of verse 24, atonement and everlasting righteousness, is accomplished at the cross, and the destruction of the city fits AD 70, so the prophecy centers wholly on Christ.

views.label: A future seventieth week
views.case: The dispensational, futurist reading sees a gap between the sixty-ninth and seventieth weeks; the church age intervenes, and the final week is a future seven-year period in which a coming prince makes and breaks a covenant and sets up the abomination, resolved only at Christ's return.

views.label: Focused on Antiochus IV
views.case: A historical reading ties the final week to the crisis under Antiochus IV in the second century BC, who halted the sacrifice and desecrated the temple, matching the abomination that causes desolation, with the Anointed One cut off understood of a high priest of that era.

debates.bearing: Across the views, the anchor holds: God decreed a set plan to deal with sin, and its saving heart, atonement and everlasting righteousness through the Anointed One, is fulfilled in Jesus; the precise chronology is held with humility.

study.originalLanguages: Daniel's whole prayer rests on one word, chesed, the covenant loving devotion of God; he pleads not Israel's righteousness but God's steadfast love and His own name. God's answer comes in seventy shavuim, seventy sevens, a symbolic span that takes up Jeremiah's seventy years and stretches them across the arc of redemption. At the center stands the promise to make kaphar, atonement, for iniquity and to bring in everlasting righteousness, the deepest aim of the whole prophecy. And the one who accomplishes it is the mashiach, the Anointed One, the Messiah and Prince, who comes and is then cut off, having nothing, so that the covering of sin might be secured for many.

typology.type: The Day of Atonement covering of sin
typology.fulfillment: The once-for-all atonement made by the Messiah cut off, to which the seventy weeks point, Hebrews 9:11-12 and 24-26.
Jesus, the Christ, anointed by the Spirit, cut off in death for the sins of many, Isaiah 53:8 and Luke 4:18.

typology.type: The ending of sacrifice and offering
typology.fulfillment: Christ's death, which ends the sacrificial system by fulfilling it, tearing the veil and opening the way to God, Matthew 27:51 and Hebrews 10:8-10.

study.christ: The seventy weeks find their saving center in the Messiah, the Prince, who is cut off but has nothing, that is, who dies not for His own sin but for the iniquity of His people. Every goal named in verse 24, to finish transgression, to put an end to sin, to make atonement for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness, is accomplished at the cross of Jesus, the Anointed One. Daniel's desperate plea, O Lord, forgive, for Your own sake, is answered here beyond all asking: God forgives not because His people are worthy but because His Son has made atonement, bringing in a righteousness that lasts forever. The prophecy draws a straight line from the ruins of exile to Calvary.
study.god: God is revealed as perfectly righteous in His judgments, so that the exile is just, and yet as the God to whom belong compassion and forgiveness, who keeps covenant love with a people who have broken faith. He hears prayer the moment it begins and sends His answer swiftly. He is not merely arranging the end of an exile but working a centuries-long plan to deal with sin at its root, and He acts, again and again, for His own name's sake, which is why guilty sinners may still hope in Him.

crossReferences.note: The seventy years and the promise of return that Daniel is reading and praying over.
The call to confess iniquity so that God will remember His covenant, the pattern of Daniel's prayer.
The Servant cut off from the land of the living for the transgression of God's people, echoed in the Messiah cut off.
Christ appearing once for all to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself, fulfilling the atonement of the seventy weeks.

study.oneStory: Daniel's confession gives voice to the whole exile: a guilty people with no plea but grace. God's answer lifts their eyes beyond the end of Babylon to the true and final deliverance, the Anointed One who finishes transgression and makes atonement for iniquity. The seventy weeks trace the line from the ruins of Jerusalem straight to the cross, where the deepest desolation of sin is met by everlasting righteousness. In Christ, cut off for many, God answers His servant's cry, O Lord, forgive, for Your own sake.

quiz.q: What prompted Daniel's prayer of confession?

options.text: Reading in Jeremiah that the seventy years of Jerusalem's desolation were nearly complete.
options.note: Daniel 9:2 shows him moved to pray by the Scriptures.

options.text: A frightening new dream he could not interpret.
options.note: This prayer was prompted by reading Jeremiah, not by a dream.

options.text: A command from King Darius to fast.
options.note: Daniel turned to prayer on his own, moved by Scripture.

options.text: A festival that required public confession.
options.note: It was his reading of Jeremiah's prophecy that led him to pray.

quiz.q: On what basis did Daniel ask God to forgive?

options.text: Not on Israel's righteousness, but on God's great compassion and for the sake of His own name.
options.note: Daniel 9:18-19 rests the plea entirely on God's mercy and name.

options.text: On the many good deeds of the exiles.
options.note: Daniel explicitly denies any appeal to their righteous acts.

options.text: On a bargain to rebuild the temple quickly.
options.note: He offered no bargain, only appeal to God's mercy.

options.text: On his own personal holiness.
options.note: He confessed sin and pleaded God's compassion, not his own merit.

quiz.q: What are the goals of the seventy weeks in verse 24?

options.text: To finish transgression, end sin, make atonement, and bring in everlasting righteousness.
options.note: Daniel 9:24 lists the sixfold saving purpose fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: Only to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem.
options.note: Rebuilding is mentioned, but the deep goal is dealing with sin forever.

options.text: To crown a new earthly king in Babylon.
options.note: The goal is atonement and righteousness, not a Babylonian coronation.

options.text: To end all prophecy and Scripture.
options.note: It seals up vision, but the heart is atonement and everlasting righteousness.

quiz.q: What does the prophecy say happens to the Messiah, the Prince?

options.text: After the appointed weeks He is cut off and has nothing, that is, He dies.
options.note: Daniel 9:26 foretells the Anointed One being cut off.

options.text: He conquers Rome and reigns from Jerusalem at once.
options.note: The prophecy foretells His being cut off, not an immediate earthly reign.

options.text: He remains hidden and never appears.
options.note: He comes and is cut off; He does appear.

options.text: He becomes king of Babylon.
options.note: He is the Anointed One who is cut off, not a Babylonian king.

quiz.q: How is the seventy weeks prophecy fulfilled in Jesus?

options.text: As the Anointed One cut off, He makes atonement for iniquity and brings in everlasting righteousness at the cross.
options.note: The sixfold goal is accomplished in Christ's death, Hebrews 9:26.

options.text: It has no fulfillment in Jesus, only in ancient politics.
options.note: The saving goals of verse 24 are fulfilled in Christ, whatever the chronology debates.

options.text: It teaches Jesus would avoid death entirely.
options.note: The opposite; the Messiah is cut off, pointing to His atoning death.

options.text: It predicts Jesus would rebuild Babylon.
options.note: It foretells atonement through the Anointed One, fulfilled at the cross.

questions: Daniel confessed not only his people's sins but joined himself to them, saying we have sinned. How does honest, humble confession shape your own prayers?
Daniel appealed to God's mercy and name, not to any goodness of his own. Where are you tempted to approach God on the basis of your record rather than His grace?
Reading Scripture moved Daniel to pray. How might letting God's word set the agenda change the way you pray?
Gabriel said the answer went out at the very beginning of Daniel's prayer. How does it encourage you to know God hears the moment you begin to pray?
The seventy weeks pointed to the Messiah who makes atonement for sin. How does resting in Christ's finished atonement free you from trying to cover your own guilt?

translationNotes.note: Loving devotion renders chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, the ground on which Daniel's whole prayer rests.
Seventy weeks may be rendered seventy sevens; the Most Holy Place may also be rendered the Most Holy One, leaving room for a personal, messianic sense.
Messiah, the Prince may be rendered the Anointed One; the title carries both royal and priestly overtones, fulfilled in Christ.
The abomination that causes desolation is a phrase Jesus takes up in Matthew 24:15; the exact chronology of the final week is much debated and held with humility.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 9:1-27. Em dashes in verses 1, 7, 8, and 20 converted to commas. Chapter 9 is Hebrew, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Hebrew. No em or en dashes used.
